10.1016/j.jmp.2021.102517
This paper combines two aspects in the construction of knowledge spaces. The first is to utilize a partial order of skills which belong to a given domain of knowledge to find items or problems which finally constitute a knowledge space or a learning space. Secondly the orders of skills of different (related) areas are coalesced by well known order operations to construct larger spaces.

The main tool employed in the first part is a so called set representation of an order (in our case, the order type of the basis of a knowledge space), i.e., a mapping of the base set of the order into the power set of a set (of problems) such that the images of this mapping ordered by set inclusion  
                         ⊆ 
                       are an isomorphic representation of the original order with the constraint that the set representing a given element of the order is strictly larger then the union of all the sets representing the predecessors, (for details see  Definition 3 ). This approach seems worth pursuing for at least two reasons. First,  Doignon (1994)  showed that an identification of the elements of the basis of a knowledge space with the skills necessary to solve the problems under scrutiny is possible and natural, i.e., we have an empirical interpretation of the basis. Second, the construction of knowledge spaces which is usually done with sophisticated, but lengthy and tedious querying procedures can be considerably simplified.

Learning spaces (well-graded knowledge spaces) have many advantages over general knowledge spaces. Our approach to knowledge space construction is particularly useful for directing the construction procedure directly towards learning spaces. In the present paper another aspect turns out to be important: the construction of a learning space which is different from an ordinal space. Ordinal spaces are in a sense the easiest knowledge spaces because they result from a rather simple prerequisite relation on the problems under consideration. Their disadvantage is however that they employ unnecessarily many problems and yield too many states. Moreover, in a complex field of knowledge a prerequisite relation on problems might not be enough to model the dependencies between them. In particular the greater complexity of a field shows in the difference between surmise systems and prerequisite relations (cf.  Falmagne & Doignon, 2011 , chapter 5). Within our set representation approach a new idea emerges: The difference between ordinal knowledge spaces and learning spaces or between surmise systems and the simpler surmise relation (which is the prerequisite relation) depends on what we call here ‘multiple items’, (see example below). The core requirement to insert multiple items are what we call ‘free fans’ which are introduced in Section  3.1 . They are a structural prerequisite and can be read off from the basis order.

Let us explain by a simple example what a multiple item is and how it works in the context of set representations. Assume the following skills are to be mastered by students of elementary arithmetic within natural numbers.

An expert will probably agree that  
                         
                            a 
                            < 
                            c 
                         
                       and  
                         
                            b 
                            < 
                            d 
                         
                       where  
                         < 
                       means “is less difficult” or more precisely “is a prerequisite for”. It is not difficult to devise items which test for each skill. Let these items be denoted 1, 2, 3, 4 for  
                         
                            a 
                            , 
                            b 
                            , 
                            c 
                            , 
                            d 
                         
                      , respectively. This choice yields the set representation  
                         a 
                       in  Fig. 2  and the ordinal knowledge space  
                         
                            
                               K 
                            
                            
                               a 
                            
                         
                       in  Fig. 3 . However, a problem asking to find the greatest common divisor of two numbers can be solved either by skill  
                         c 
                       or by skill  
                         d 
                       because the  
                         
                            g 
                            c 
                            d 
                            
                               ( 
                               x 
                               , 
                               y 
                               )

                            x 
                            ∕ 
                            y 
                         
                      . However, finding the  
                         
                            g 
                            c 
                            d 
                         
                       can be done by finding prime factors or by the Euclidean algorithm, which amounts to perform repeatedly division with remainder. The  
                         
                            g 
                            c 
                            d 
                         
                      -problem is an example of a multiple item. Inserting it in a test together with an item for skill  
                         a 
                       and an item for skill  
                         b 
                       gives the set representation  
                         b 
                       in  Fig. 2  and the knowledge space  
                         
                            
                               K 
                            
                            
                               b 
                            
                         
                       in  Fig. 3 . In this space 3 is a multiple item.

No doubt, everybody who has constructed knowledge spaces in practice by applying, for example, a query procedure will have found many instances of multiple items; mostly, probably without paying attention to their role.

In the second part, i.e., in Section  4  the techniques to “add” and “multiply” orders are applied to the basis orders resulting in new orders to which the set representation theory of the first part is applied. Properties of the building blocks are identified which can be transferred to the combination and yield properties of the final knowledge space which make their handling a bit more convenient. Of particular interest is how the all important free fans behave under these operations. Mostly, free fans of the building blocks show up in the final order. More than that, several new cases are generated by these operations (cf.  Section  4.3 ).

The concept of a set representation in connection with knowledge space theory was introduced and investigated in  Suck (2003, 2004, 2011) .  Ganter, Bedek, Heller, and Suck (2017)  contains also a description of this aspect. In the present paper the focus is on how to exploit this perspective on knowledge spaces for their construction. In this respect the concepts of multiple items and free fans ( Definition 12 ) seem new. In the course of this investigation some apparently new results are presented, in particular in connection with the order operations.

We recall some notions from order theory and add a few special ones which are needed in the sequel. A reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive binary relation  
                            ≤ 
                          on a set  
                            P 
                         , is called a partial order, often abridged ‘poset’, and denoted  
                            
                               ( 
                               P 
                               , 
                               ≤ 
                               ) 
                            
                         . The denotations  
                            
                               〈 
                               , 
                               ≥ 
                               , 
                               〉 
                            
                          are used in the usual way. A subset of  
                            P 
                         , say  
                            X 
                         , gives rise to an induced suborder  
                            
                               ( 
                               X 
                               , 
                               
                                  
                                     ≤ 
                                  
                                  
                                     X 
                                  
                               
                               ) 
                            
                         , meaning  
                            
                               x 
                               
                                  
                                     ≤ 
                                  
                                  
                                     X 
                                  
                               
                               y 
                            
                          whenever  
                            
                               x 
                               , 
                               y 
                               ∈ 
                               X 
                            
                          and  
                            
                               x 
                               ≤ 
                               y 
                            
                         . To facilitate notation we will denote  
                            
                               
                                  ≤ 
                               
                               
                                  X 
                               
                            
                          simply by  
                            ≤ 
                          when no confusion can arise. A quasiorder (often called preorder, in particular in the economic literature) is a reflexive and transitive relation. If we denote a quasiorder by  
                            ≾ 
                          then  
                            
                               a 
                               ≾ 
                               b 
                            
                          and  
                            
                               b 
                               ≾ 
                               a 
                            
                          is denoted by  
                            
                               a 
                               ∼ 
                               b 
                            
                         . Clearly,  
                            ∼ 
                          is an equivalence relation.

Next we introduce two ways to ‘add’ two partial orders, namely parallel addition and serial addition.

                                     P 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                            
                         . In Section  4  we will encounter two more operations of combining orders to yield a new one.

It is well known that a knowledge space  
                         K 
                       is completely determined by its basis  
                         B 
                      , i.e., a minimal subset of  
                         K 
                       such that each  
                         
                            K 
                            ∈ 
                            K 
                         
                       is the union of sets in  
                         B 
                      . There are sets  
                         
                            
                               
                                  B 
                               
                               
                                  1 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            … 
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  B 
                               
                               
                                  k 
                               
                            
                            ∈ 
                            B 
                         
                       depending on  
                         K 
                       with  
                         
                            K 
                            = 
                            
                               
                                  B 
                               
                               
                                  1 
                               
                            
                            ∪ 
                            ⋯ 
                            ∪ 
                            
                               
                                  B 
                               
                               
                                  k 
                               
                            
                         
                      . Moreover, none of the sets in  
                         B 
                       can be obtained as union of other sets in  
                         K 
                      . In lattice theory such elements are known as ‘join irreducible’ (see for example  Grätzer (1998)  or  Davey and Priestley (2002)  for a general reference to lattice theory). For distributive lattices the order of the join irreducibles determines the lattice. For arbitrary lattices this order contains some of its properties but not all. However, in addition to the combinatorial importance of the basis we emphasize the material interpretation in the context of knowledge spaces: The basis can be identified with a (minimal) set of skills necessary to master a field of knowledge. This aspect was pointed out by  Doignon (1994)  and is worked out in Chapter 6 of  Falmagne and Doignon (2011) .

To illustrate the role of the basis we give in  Fig. 1  four knowledge spaces. The bases of all four are isomorphic as orders  
                         
                            ( 
                            B 
                            , 
                            ⊆ 
                            ) 
                         
                      , namely  
                         1 + 1 + 1 
                      , however the knowledge spaces look rather different.

To capture the additional information in a knowledge space (which is in fact a lattice) we introduce the notion of a set representation of an order  
                            
                               ( 
                               P 
                               , 
                               ≤ 
                               ) 
                            
                         , i.e., an order homomorphism where the image order is  
                            
                               ( 
                               
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                                  
                                     Q 
                                  
                               
                               , 
                               ⊆ 
                               ) 
                            
                          for some set  
                            Q 
                         . The restriction of this order to the basis  
                            
                               B 
                            
                          of  
                            K 
                          is isomorphic to  
                            
                               ( 
                               P 
                               , 
                               ≤ 
                               ) 
                            
                         . However, we do not start with  
                            
                               ( 
                               B 
                               , 
                               ⊆ 
                               ) 
                            
                          but rather with the order type of it. That is, we start with an abstract order  
                            
                               ( 
                               P 
                               , 
                               ≤ 
                               ) 
                            
                          which in the intended application is isomorphic to  
                            
                               ( 
                               B 
                               , 
                               ⊆ 
                               )

                            φ 
                          into  
                            
                               ( 
                               
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                                  
                                     Q 
                                  
                               
                               , 
                               ⊆ 
                               ) 
                            
                         .

A special PSR is easily obtained by taking the set  
                            P 
                          as the set  
                            Q 
                          and utilizing the principal ideals of

                               P 
                               , 
                               ≤ 
                               )

                          is clearly a PSR. In general there are more PSRs and even more merely basic set representations. The importance of a PSR for knowledge space theory is pointed out in  Suck (2003) . There it was shown that well-gradedness of a knowledge space is equivalent to parsimony of the set representation of its abstract order. More precisely, if  
                            
                               ( 
                               B 
                               , 
                               ⊆ 
                               ) 
                            
                          is the basis of a knowledge space  
                            K 
                          and  
                            
                               ( 
                               
                                  
                                     P 
                                  
                                  
                                     B 
                                  
                               
                               , 
                               ≤ 
                               ) 
                            
                          an abstract partial order isomorphic to  
                            
                               ( 
                               B 
                               , 
                               ⊆ 
                               ) 
                            
                          then the isomorphism is a PSR of  
                            
                               ( 
                               
                                  
                                     P 
                                  
                                  
                                     B 
                                  
                               
                               , 
                               ≤ 
                               ) 
                            
                          if and only if  
                            K 
                          is well-graded.

Let  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     K 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        P 
                                        , 
                                        ≤ 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                         denote the set of all knowledge spaces with basis order isomorphic to  
                            
                               ( 
                               P 
                               , 
                               ≤ 
                               ) 
                            
                         . Much of the present paper is concerned with describing  
                            
                               K 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  P 
                                  , 
                                  ≤ 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                          with the help of the properties of the respective  
                            φ 
                         .  Fig. 3  shows the knowledge spaces of the representations in  Fig. 2 .

We derive a result which shows that the number of states resulting from the principal ideal representation is greater than or equal to the number of states of a knowledge space ensuing from an arbitrary PSR. Moreover, we demonstrate how this property can be exploited.

Do the spaces in  Fig. 3  give the whole set  
                            
                               K 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  2 + 2 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                         ? Obviously not, because we can always add further elements to  
                            Q 
                         . However, the ensuing spaces are not essentially different. To further analyse this phenomenon and to describe  
                            
                               K 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  P 
                                  , 
                                  ≤ 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                          better we introduce the notion of reducibility.

In  Suck (2004)  several properties of saturated orders are provided, among them necessary and sufficient conditions. In the present paper we stress violations of saturation because for not saturated orders it is possible to find problem sets  
                            Q 
                          with less than  
                            
                               | 
                               P 
                               | 
                            
                          elements. Needing fewer problems is a benefit because a student has to work on fewer problems or questions; however, on a deeper level one can show that the learning space will be smaller (cf.   Theorem 8 ). This fact does not follow, in general, from the smaller number of items but rather from the properties of the chosen set representation. These beneficial problems are called multiple items and introduced in the following definition.

                            ⊆

                            q 
                         . For example, problem 3 in  Fig. 2 b is a multiple item. This structure formalizes the example of the introduction with 3 the gcd-problem. The principal ideal representation does not provide any multiple items. Hence, in  Fig. 2 a there is no multiple item. In  Fig. 2 c problems 3 and 4 are multiple, in  Figs. 2 d and  2 e 3 is a multiple item. We argue that multiple items are important because they can simplify a knowledge space when used properly, at least for PSRs.

If a knowledge space is constructed the usual way by query procedures or derived from solution data in a sample or population multiple items can be detected from the sets  
                            
                               
                                  K 
                               
                               
                                  q 
                               
                            
                          of states of  
                            K 
                          which contain  
                            q 
                         . An item

                         -multiple if  
                            
                               ( 
                               
                                  
                                     K 
                                  
                                  
                                     q 
                                  
                               
                               , 
                               ⊆ 
                               ) 
                            
                          has  
                            k 
                          minima.

However, PSRs are not always superior to other representations. They need less problems but not necessarily less states. Examples are  
                            
                               
                                  K 
                               
                               
                                  3 
                               
                            
                          in  Fig. 1  and  
                            
                               
                                  K 
                               
                               
                                  c

                            n 
                          elements it becomes even more striking. That is, we set  
                            
                               P 
                               = 
                               
                                  {

                               , 
                               ≤

                            n

                                  2 
                               
                               
                                  P 
                               
                            
                          as knowledge space. Thus, when the set representation is not parsimonious the space can be rather small. This observation is however not a plea against PSRs because the well-graded spaces (which as pointed out above result from PSRs) are more in tune with learning processes than the general spaces. That is the reason for the name learning space.

Finally, we want to point out that the difference  
                            
                               
                                  | 
                                  P 
                                  | 
                               
                               − 
                               
                                  | 
                                  S 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     P 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  | 
                               
                            
                          is the scope by which we can reduce the number of items by resorting to multiple items. The next section deals with structural properties of the skill order which suggest the use of multiple items.

The examples in the previous sections where multiple items could be exploited point to particular suborders of the skill order. In all cases parallel suborders existed and gave rise to the possibility of introducing a multiple item. It is easy to see that parallel suborders facilitate the use of multiple items; however they are neither necessary nor sufficient. Examples given in  Suck (2011)  show that parallel suborders might exist where multiple items cannot be introduced. On the other hand, we shall see that parallelism is too strong. In this section we will clarify what is required. In addition to  Definition 1  we introduce several concepts related to partial orders.

Another way to say that  
                         X 
                       is a fan is that it is a suborder of height 2 with exactly one maximal element all of whose direct precursors constitute its minimal elements. The condition that all elements covered by the maximum are in the suborder is essential for our purpose. Because of this condition every  
                         
                            p 
                            ∈ 
                            P 
                            − 
                            min 
                            P 
                         
                       generates a unique fan, namely  
                         
                            
                               F 
                            
                            
                               p 
                            
                         
                       consisting of  
                         p 
                       and the elements covered by  
                         p 
                      . Weakly parallel fans are not parallel, however, their maxima are parallel and at least one element in  
                         
                            min 
                            
                               
                                  F 
                               
                               
                                  1 
                               
                            
                         
                       is parallel to the one element in  
                         
                            max 
                            
                               
                                  F 
                               
                               
                                  2 
                               
                            
                         
                       or vice versa.

Fans are essential for the question of multiple items because of the properties of basic set representations. To decide whether an item can become a multiple item for elements  
                         
                            p 
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  ′

                            P

                         p 
                       and of

                               ′

                         p 
                       or  
                         
                            
                               p 
                            
                            
                               ′

                               p

                            p 
                         
                       holds. This must not happen because of the properties of a basic set representation. The following subsections clarify this state of affairs.

The investigation of a given partial order can be done by looking at the structure of fans. This is in particular true of the question of finding places where multiple items can be employed. Before working out the exact properties we note an obvious result.

Proposition 11 
                            
                               
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     F 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        P 
                                        , 
                                        ≤ 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     , 
                                     ≾ 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                
                               is a partial order. The relation  
                               
                                  ↗ 
                                
                               is in general not transitive. Moreover,  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           F 
                                        
                                        
                                           1 
                                        
                                     
                                     ≾ 
                                     
                                        
                                           F 
                                        
                                        
                                           2 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                
                               implies

Proof 
                            The antisymmetry of  
                                  ≾ 
                                on  
                                  F 
                                follows from the fact that

                                        max

                                     = 
                                     1 
                                  
                                and antisymmetry of  
                                  ≤ 
                                on  
                                  P 
                               ; transitivity follows similarly. (Note that a fan is uniquely determined by its maximal element). The other statements of this proposition are even more trivial. □

In  
                            G 
                          vertices, i.e., non-minimal elements are connected if the corresponding fans are free.  Theorem 13 
                         2.  suggests that the cliques of this graph, i.e., sets of vertices which are completely connected, are important for the construction of parsimonious set representations which exploit multiple items whenever possible.

The technique of the proof of  Theorem 13 b works when we start with the principal ideal representation and proceed until a maximal clique is dealt with. But the graph has in most cases more cliques. Can we exhaust the whole graph by working from one maximal clique to the next? The answer is negative. In  Fig. 6  we have an example of a simple partial order with many pairs of free graphs and the corresponding free fan graph. Except for the isolated vertex  
                            
                               
                                  F 
                               
                               
                                  c 
                               
                            
                          the graph is bipartite because of the special structure of this example.  
                            
                               
                                  F 
                               
                               
                                  c 
                               
                            
                          is isolated because for  
                            
                               b 
                               , 
                               d 
                               , 
                               e 
                               , 
                               f 
                            
                         
                         Definition 12 
                         1.  is violated and for  
                            
                               h 
                               , 
                               i 
                               , 
                               j 
                               , 
                               k 
                               , 
                               l 
                            
                         
                         2.  of this definition is not satisfied. The minimal number of items needed for  
                            
                               ( 
                               P 
                               , 
                               ≤ 
                               ) 
                            
                          is  
                            
                               S 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  P 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                         . This number is possibly hard to achieve. However, a procedure for the purpose of constructing an optimal set representation emerges.

1. 
                               Construct the free fan graph  
                                     G 
                                  . 
                            
                            
                               2. 
                               Find the cliques of  
                                     G 
                                  . 
                            
                            
                               3. 
                               Choose a subset of cliques such that a maximal number of vertices of  
                                     G 
                                   are contained in exactly one clique. 
                            
                            
                               4. 
                               Apply  Theorem 15  to this set.

Construct the free fan graph  
                                     G 
                                  .

Find the cliques of  
                                     G 
                                  .

Choose a subset of cliques such that a maximal number of vertices of  
                                     G 
                                   are contained in exactly one clique.

Apply  Theorem 15  to this set.

The graph  
                            G 
                          may contain isolated vertices. These fans cannot be exploited by multiple items. Moreover, some vertices may not be contained in any of our chosen cliques. These can also not be exploited. But the other cliques can contribute to saving items by introducing a multiple item per clique. If the clique has  
                            k 
                          vertices such an item must be a  
                            k 
                         -multiple item. However, our choice of cliques may not be optimal. It seems advantageous to select maximal cliques because in each clique locally the benefit is maximal. However, overall there might be better choices of cliques. Apparently, we are here confronted with a non trivial optimization problem.

In practical applications of knowledge space theory the construction of smaller ‘units’ which later can be combined to form a larger space has proven useful. The concept of the mesh of knowledge spaces originated from this desideratum (cf.  Falmagne & Doignon, 2011 , ch. 7 on the concept of the mesh and meshability of knowledge structures). In the context of the present paper there are several rather simple and straightforward techniques to meet such a requirement. In keeping with our skills first strategy we do not work with the final spaces but rather with the abstract orders of the bases of the spaces. After combining these by one of the well known operations listed in  Definitions 2 and 16  we can come up with combined spaces with the help of suitably chosen set representations. Depending on these mappings we can have desirable properties such as well-gradedness and can by exploiting multiple items aim for smaller final spaces. Moreover, the interpretation of the results in terms of skills is more or less straightforward given the skill orders of the original spaces.

Definition 2  introduced two additions of orders. There are also two multiplications given in the following definition. Here again a new order is derived from orders  
                         
                            ( 
                            
                               
                                  P 
                               
                               
                                  1 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  ≤ 
                               
                               
                                  1 
                               
                            
                            ) 
                         
                       and  
                         
                            ( 
                            
                               
                                  P 
                               
                               
                                  2 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  ≤ 
                               
                               
                                  2 
                               
                            
                            ) 
                         
                      :

Fig. 7  gives the Cartesian and the lexicographic product of the same two orders. With the help of  Propositions 17 and 18  it should be easy to construct these covering graphs. Another example of a lexicographic product is the order in  Fig. 6 . Can the reader see the factors?

The operations of  Definitions 2 and 16  presuppose disjoint base sets. Parallel addition and Cartesian product are commutative operations. For serial addition and lexicographic product the sequence in which the orders are combined is essential.

In Section  2  it was argued that the basis of a knowledge space has an empirical meaning because the base elements can be identified with the skills of a field of knowledge. Consequently, the order operations should have an impact on this interpretation because they are intended to be applied to the order of the basis. Prior to combining we have two disjoint sets of skills. We discuss briefly how the interpretation of the components influences the interpretation after ‘summation’ or ‘multiplication’. For each operation we sketch a potential example.

Skills of both knowledge areas are completely independent, nevertheless depending upon the set representation the ensuing knowledge space may not show this independence directly. Skills can be acquired in separate curricula, not in a predetermined sequence. An example could be learning elementary arithmetic and orthography. Together, both fields reflect much of the abilities elementary school children are supposed to acquire.

The skills of the first space are a strict prerequisite of the skills of the second space. The skills for addition and multiplication of integers form a pertinent example.

The product skills are pairs of skills. The student has always one skill of each field of knowledge. If he or she has only skills of one of the fields then no item of the combined space can be solved. The skills must be learned in pairs. Both areas contribute together and to the same extent to the progress. When teaching a course in mathematical psychology the students are meant to acquire skills in mathematics and in psychology. The items of a knowledge space should all contain both aspects. Similarly, physical chemistry or biophysics provide examples.

Here too, the skills come in pairs. However, if a student has a high skill in the first field of knowledge he or she fares well provided he or she has at least one of the skills of the second field. The skills must be learned in pairs. However, one field contributes more to the progress than the other. Here an example is the solution of word problems in elementary school. It requires skills in equation solving and calculating ( 
                               
                                  
                                     P 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                            ) on the mathematical side and some amount of text comprehension ( 
                               
                                  
                                     P 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                            ) on the linguistic side. Arguably, a knowledge space may be  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        P 
                                     
                                     
                                        1 
                                     
                                  
                                  ⊗ 
                                  
                                     
                                        P 
                                     
                                     
                                        2 
                                     
                                  
                               
                            .

In the way we introduced the operations here we need disjoint sets. In practice, this may be not always possible or warranted. For example, skills for mastering reading and writing may partly overlap. We did not investigate this possibility with respect to order operations.

In  Suck (2004)  several results are derived concerning the behaviour of the saturation index of the combined order given the respective indices of the factors. We summarize them here.

                                     = 
                                     ∅ 
                                  
                               
                               . Then 
                            
                            
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        (a)

                                              max

Examples show that the estimations in  3. and 4.  cannot be improved. On the other hand it is possible that even in the case  
                            
                               S 
                               
                                  (

                               =

                                        i 
                                     
                                  
                                  | 
                               
                            
                          (implying no multiple items in the set representations of  
                            
                               ( 
                               
                                  
                                     P 
                                  
                                  
                                     i 
                                  
                               
                               , 
                               
                                  
                                     ≤ 
                                  
                                  
                                     i 
                                  
                               
                               ) 
                            
                         ) there can be multiple items in the products. For an example the reader is invited to verify  
                            
                               S 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  3 × 3 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               8 
                               < 
                               S

                                  3

                               S 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  3

                         . Thus, the possibility of one multiple item exists in the product.

The usefulness of the index  
                            
                               S 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  P 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                          can be seen from Theorem 6 in  Suck (2004)  from which another sufficient condition for the existence of multiple items can be derived. Note that a partial order  
                            
                               ( 
                               P 
                               , 
                               ≤ 
                               ) 
                            
                          is a  Cartesian prime  if there are no nontrivial orders  
                            
                               ( 
                               
                                  
                                     P 
                                  
                                  
                                     i 
                                  
                               
                               , 
                               
                                  
                                     ≤ 
                                  
                                  
                                     i 
                                  
                               
                               ) 
                            
                          such that  
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  P 
                                  , 
                                  ≤ 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  
                                     
                                        P 
                                     
                                     
                                        1 
                                     
                                  
                                  × 
                                  
                                     
                                        P 
                                     
                                     
                                        2 
                                     
                                  
                                  , 
                                  ≤ 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                         . In  Suck (2004)  it is proved that a saturated order is an antichain, a grid of the form  
                            2 × n 
                          or a Cartesian prime. Thus, when an order is not an antichain, not of the form  
                            2 × n 
                         , and not a Cartesian prime then it is not saturated. Consequently, there are set representations with multiple items. Finding and utilizing them makes the knowledge space smaller in two respects: Less problems and less states.

The operations of  Definitions 2 and 16  have the potential to create places for multiple items. For the two products the situation is more complicated than for the additions. We summarize the results for the four operations. The reader is reminded that

                          denotes the unique fan with maximum  
                            p 
                          for

                               min 
                               P 
                            
                         .

This case is a little more complicated. For free fans  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        F 
                                     
                                     
                                        p 
                                     
                                  
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        F 
                                     
                                     
                                        q 
                                     
                                  
                               
                             of an arbitrary order condition  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        F 
                                     
                                     
                                        p 
                                     
                                  
                                  ⇕ 
                                  
                                     
                                        F 
                                     
                                     
                                        q 
                                     
                                  
                               
                             necessarily implies  
                               
                                  p 
                                  ∥ 
                                  q 
                               
                            . For a Cartesian product this condition is almost sufficient. To clarify the situation the distinction of two classes of fans of the Cartesian product according to  Proposition 17  is useful.

Definition 20 
                               A fan  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              F 
                                           
                                           
                                              
                                                 ( 
                                                 p 
                                                 , 
                                                 x 
                                                 ) 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                        ∈ 
                                        F 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           
                                              
                                                 P 
                                              
                                              
                                                 1 
                                              
                                           
                                           × 
                                           
                                              
                                                 P 
                                              
                                              
                                                 2 
                                              
                                           
                                           , 
                                           ≤ 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                     
                                   is of type 1 if  
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        p 
                                        , 
                                        x 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                   covers only elements of the form  
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        p 
                                        , 
                                        y 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                   or  
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        q 
                                        , 
                                        x 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                   in  
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        
                                           
                                              P 
                                           
                                           
                                              1 
                                           
                                        
                                        × 
                                        
                                           
                                              P 
                                           
                                           
                                              2 
                                           
                                        
                                        , 
                                        ≤ 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                  , but not both. It is a fan of type 2 if  
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        p 
                                        , 
                                        x 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                   covers elements of the form  
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        p 
                                        , 
                                        y 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                   and  
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        q 
                                        , 
                                        x 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                  .

A fan  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              F 
                                           
                                           
                                              
                                                 ( 
                                                 p 
                                                 , 
                                                 x 
                                                 ) 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                        ∈ 
                                        F 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           
                                              
                                                 P 
                                              
                                              
                                                 1 
                                              
                                           
                                           × 
                                           
                                              
                                                 P 
                                              
                                              
                                                 2 
                                              
                                           
                                           , 
                                           ≤ 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                     
                                   is of type 1 if  
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        p 
                                        , 
                                        x 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                   covers only elements of the form  
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        p 
                                        , 
                                        y 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                   or  
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        q 
                                        , 
                                        x 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                   in  
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        
                                           
                                              P 
                                           
                                           
                                              1 
                                           
                                        
                                        × 
                                        
                                           
                                              P 
                                           
                                           
                                              2 
                                           
                                        
                                        , 
                                        ≤ 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                  , but not both. It is a fan of type 2 if  
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        p 
                                        , 
                                        x 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                   covers elements of the form  
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        p 
                                        , 
                                        y 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                   and  
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        q 
                                        , 
                                        x 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                  .

How can we detect whether a fan is of type 1 or 2? In terms of the component orders we prove the following criterion:

Proposition 21 
                               
                                  A fan  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           F 
                                        
                                        
                                           
                                              ( 
                                              p 
                                              , 
                                              x 
                                              ) 
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                   
                                  is of type 1 iff either  
                                  
                                     p

In summary the structure of free fans and the graph

                             is more complicated than for the two addition operations.

The characterization of free fans in the lexicographic product takes a bit of preparation. We begin with a definition of a partition of the set of pairs of non-minimal elements (which corresponds to the set of pairs of fans):

The proof shows that the complications with free fans in the lexicographic product are mainly due to the minimal elements of

The incentive to approach knowledge space theory in the way expounded in this paper stems from two observations:

• 
                            The first is the paper  Doignon (1994)  which identifies the elements of the base of a knowledge space with the skills of a field and introduces the concept of a skill map. Thus, an empirical interpretation of the base suggests itself. The present paper, in a way, reverses the procedure; it suggests to start with the skills, not to extract them, and to search for or formulate the items or problems accordingly. Multiple items and free fans introduced here are the tools to do this construction efficiently. 
                         
                         
                            • 
                            The second is an underlying correspondence between a lattice (the knowledge space) and its join-irreducible elements (the basis). In lattice theory it is common knowledge that there is a ‘partnership’ between the two, at least for distributive lattices (cf.  Davey & Priestley, 2002 , ch. 5; the correspondence between finite distributive lattices and ordered sets is called ‘the distributive-lattice theorist’s logarithm’). Clearly, knowledge spaces are not necessarily distributive lattices, but quasi-ordinal ones are and well-graded knowledge spaces are join-distributive. Hence, we can profit from this correspondence in knowledge space theory. The present paper explores to what extent this profit can be manifest.

The first is the paper  Doignon (1994)  which identifies the elements of the base of a knowledge space with the skills of a field and introduces the concept of a skill map. Thus, an empirical interpretation of the base suggests itself. The present paper, in a way, reverses the procedure; it suggests to start with the skills, not to extract them, and to search for or formulate the items or problems accordingly. Multiple items and free fans introduced here are the tools to do this construction efficiently.

The second is an underlying correspondence between a lattice (the knowledge space) and its join-irreducible elements (the basis). In lattice theory it is common knowledge that there is a ‘partnership’ between the two, at least for distributive lattices (cf.  Davey & Priestley, 2002 , ch. 5; the correspondence between finite distributive lattices and ordered sets is called ‘the distributive-lattice theorist’s logarithm’). Clearly, knowledge spaces are not necessarily distributive lattices, but quasi-ordinal ones are and well-graded knowledge spaces are join-distributive. Hence, we can profit from this correspondence in knowledge space theory. The present paper explores to what extent this profit can be manifest.

In summary, the process of learning space construction in the set representation framework has to implement the following steps.

First, one has to find the skills of the field of knowledge for which the knowledge space is developed. Second, one has to determine the partial order of these skills, denote it  
                         
                            ( 
                            P 
                            , 
                            ≤ 
                            ) 
                         
                      . Third, this order should be investigated with respect to sets of free fans, i.e., the graph  
                         G 
                       has to be constructed. Fourth, an optimal subset of its cliques has to be found according to the requirements of Section  3.3 . Fifth, multiple items for each of these cliques have to be found or constructed; this might require some ingenuity.

If, in this way, a set  
                         Q 
                       of items with  
                         
                            
                               | 
                               Q 
                               | 
                            
                            = 
                            S 
                            
                               ( 
                               P 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                       can be found such that a parsimonious set representation  
                         
                            φ 
                            : 
                            
                            P 
                            → 
                            
                               
                                  2 
                               
                               
                                  Q 
                               
                            
                         
                       can be constructed one has the best possible parsimonious set representation. As a result, the ensuing knowledge space is well-graded and encompasses a minimum number of problems. Moreover, the number of states is also minimal for this skill order when only learning spaces are considered.

If the field of knowledge for which the space is developed can be partitioned in subfields then proceeding in each field separately is possible. The results of Section  4  suggest to combine the skill orders of the subfields with one of the operations described in this section and apply the third step — investigating for free fans and searching for multiple items — afterwards because the combined orders have in most cases more possibilities for multiple items.

It should be mentioned that skills in connection with knowledge spaces are around in other approaches. Starting with  Korossy (1997)  a development emerged that built skill or competence spaces parallel to knowledge spaces. This led to numerous applications and theoretical developments, mostly under the so called ‘competence-based knowledge space theory’ championed by a research group in Graz (cf. Part II of  Falmagne, Albert, Doble, Eppstein, & Hu, 2013 , chapters 9–13). The link between the two areas, skills and items/problems, is described by so-called skill functions and problem function. These were theoretically investigated in  Gediga and Düntsch (2002) . Further developments are  Heller, Anselmi, Stefanutti, and Robusto (2017), Heller, Ünlü, and Albert (2013)  and many more. The idea presented here with set representations differs from this approach mainly insofar as with set representations the two areas are more directly intertwined.

One of the reviewers of the first version of this paper argued that multiple items can cause difficulties, in particular, in the presence of careless errors and lucky guesses. There is no definitive answer to this question. From an information theoretic point of view the result of  Theorem 8  shows that the solution a student presents to a given problem in  
                         Q 
                       contains more information in a learning space than in an ordinal space (depending however on the probability distribution on the states of  
                         K 
                      ). Hence, multiple item are beneficial. On the other hand, more items used to represent a skill order might increase redundancy. In this respect, many multiple items can make assessment procedures more inaccurate, in particular, if lucky guesses and careless errors are likely to occur. Clearly, an investigation of these questions for example in connection with assessment algorithms described in Falmagne & Doignon, 201,) ch. 13 seems worthwhile pursuing.

There are some more open problems in our approach. One is related to the role we assigned to cliques in Section  3.3 . Cliques in a graph keep being a much investigated topic in graph theory. Clustering algorithms are often related to cliques in graphs. Many algorithmically demanding problems are connected with these investigations. There are many applications in quite different fields, for an example in genetics we mention  Ben-Dor, Shamir, and Yakhini (1999) . The suggestion to choose a set of cliques in order to apply multiple items as efficient as possible may run into complexity problems. Another open problem looming behind our investigation is connected to the question what is the largest number of elements of a base of a knowledge space on  
                         Q 
                       in terms of  
                         
                            | 
                            Q 
                            | 
                         
                       (cf.  Falmagne & Doignon, 2011 , ch. 18). In the present paper it can be asked in an inverted form: What is the smallest number of  
                         
                            | 
                            Q 
                            | 
                         
                       such that a given order is the basis order of a knowledge space on  
                         Q 
                      ?
